channel.title: cloudwatch on The musings of Mister Davido
channel.description: Recent content in cloudwatch on The musings of Mister Davido

item.title: Part 4/4 - Create and deploy an automated, serverless Newegg stock checker using Python, Lambda, and Terraform
item.description: Alright.. the home stretch. At the end of the last part, we mentioned that we need a stateful data store for our Lambda function, otherwise it would keep wanting to send alerts every minute that there’s no stock, and that’s not very useful. The stateful data store we’re going to use is S3. Of course, you don’t have to use S3 - you could spin up a DynamoDB or RDS instance, and there’s the relatively new EFS on Lambda which I haven’t personally tested, but for our use case - storing an arbitrary file - S3 is my pick.

item.title: Part 3/4 - Create and deploy an automated, serverless Newegg stock checker using Python, Lambda, and Terraform
item.description: We’ve got a working Lambda function now, so let’s start adding in the other pieces of the AWS puzzle to turn this into a lean, mean, automated stock-checking machine. How often should we run it? Very good question. For a hot product that may literally last a few minutes on the “shelf”, it sounds like we’d want to check pretty frequently. Ideally within timeframes of a minute. But there’s one all-important thing to consider that holds true more than anything else in AWS, or cloud computing in general.
